text: The posterior third robotic arm is used to retract tissue superiorly, enhancing the surgical field visibility.
The surgeon uses a curved bipolar dissector to manipulate tissue within the surgical field.

The surgeon provides extra traction on the tissue to facilitate cutting with the instrument, ensuring adequate tension to prevent burning. The level nine lymph node is excised using a combination of retraction and timely energy application.
The surgeon allows the lung to drop towards the diaphragm and then retracts it anteriorly, using the posterior arm to push while coordinating with the assistant.

The assistant retracts the lower aspect of the lower lobe using the posterior robotic twelve trocar, while the surgeon retracts high towards the head, allowing for two-handed dissection of the posterior hilum.
The surgeon mobilizes the lower lobe of the lung, carefully separating it from the hilar structures using surgical instruments.
The surgeon exposes the vein, bronchus, and pulmonary artery, preparing to dissect the level seven nodal station.
The surgeon uses spreading and pushing techniques to expose the vessels that need to be addressed, revealing the anatomical structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully peels off the pleural covering over the lymph nodes, revealing the underlying structures.
The surgeon isolates and prepares to individually dissect the visible vessels, including the vein, bronchus, and pulmonary artery, near the level seven nodal station.
The surgeon gently grasps the partially mobilized node, carefully holding it without pulling, to access the underside and use electrocautery to burn and detach the remaining tissue.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the node without grabbing it prematurely to avoid tearing, while continuing the dissection to mobilize the node further.
The surgeon carefully frees the surrounding tissue around the node, ensuring it is adequately mobilized before attempting to grasp it.
The surgeon completes dissection in the subcarinal region, approaching the area near the level of the L structure.
The surgeon removes the node, allowing for control of any residual bleeding in the surgical field.
The surgeon moves up to the hilar region and begins excising the lymph nodes at level XL, carefully separating them from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon adjusts the position of the retracting hand to a higher location, likely to improve access or visibility in the surgical field.
The surgeon's assistant retracts lower, allowing the surgeon to use both hands to approach and dissect the level ten L lymph node, located near the pulmonary artery and bronchus.
The surgeon mobilizes the edges of the thymus, carefully freeing the pleural envelope surrounding it after unroofing the tissue.
The surgeon performs a bimanual dissection, utilizing both hands to manipulate and separate tissue around the level ten L lymph node, with assistance providing retraction for optimal access.
The surgeon focuses on mobilizing the lymph nodes, carefully freeing them from surrounding tissue to achieve optimal exposure.
The surgeon opens the tissue planes around the vessels, creating clear windows that facilitate easier access and manipulation of the vessels.
The surgeon identifies the posterior segment branch, labeled as A-II, on the left side, highlighting its anatomical position during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon mobilizes the lymph nodes and creates an exit point or window around the vessel to reduce obstruction for the stapler.

The surgeon applies aggressive forward retraction to enter the correct anatomical plane, aiming to skeletonize the vessel. As the dissection progresses, the anterior branch of the artery becomes visible, with tissue being cleared around the top of the artery to facilitate access to the first branch.
The surgeon continues to address the hilar and aortopulmonary (APO) window lymph nodes, which are visible and accessible for further dissection.
The surgeon navigates around the posterior segment branch, identified as A-II on the left side, after completing previous steps to mobilize lymph nodes and create an unobstructed pathway.
A small amount of residual tissue remains visible around the vessel, requiring further dissection or removal.
The surgeon uses a sponge as a dissector to manipulate and remove tissue, or alternatively cuts the tissue directly.
The surgeon approaches the branch from the anterior trocar site, specifically the twelfth trocar, which is positioned as the lowermost anterior trocar.
The surgeon spreads the instrument during dissection, revealing the reflection of the pulmonary artery (PA) in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a bovie electrocautery device to carefully dissect through a very thin fissure, revealing underlying anatomical structures.
The video shows a thicker fissure in a different patient, highlighting the anatomical variation in the fissure thickness compared to previous cases.
The surgeon spreads the tissue to reveal the pulmonary artery, checking for visibility and anatomical orientation.
The surgeon frees the tissue to create a tunnel, completing the dissection of the fissure posteriorly.
The surgeon carefully aims slightly upwards, ensuring the dissection remains above the artery, avoiding the inferior edge during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses the right hand to retract tissue with a hook-like motion, while the left hand actively spreads and manipulates the tissue in the surgical field.
The surgeon creates a window by adjusting the dissection plane higher towards the head, revealing a clearer anatomical plane above the artery.
The surgeon prepares to use a stapler, ensuring it is positioned in a safe opening to avoid injury to nearby vessels or the lung. The tissue is encircled, and a short brush of tissue is visible.
A 12mm trocar is inserted from the anterior approach, providing access for the surgical instruments during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon exposes the pulmonary artery, with the posterior segment artery clearly isolated. The posterior fissure is divided, revealing a small posterior ascending branch.
The surgeon uses a stapler to divide a small vessel, ensuring smooth alignment to avoid jerky movements. Once the vessel is divided, the posterior fissure is fully exposed.

The surgeon exposes the posterior segment branch and begins mobilizing the surrounding lymph nodes. A larger, more typical-sized branch is visible, and dissection is performed around the exit point, creating a window superiorly past the vessel.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection to enlarge the surgical window, carefully separating tissue layers to improve access and visibility.
The surgeon cleans connective tissue around a vessel, ensuring a clear window for instrument insertion. The vessel loop is engaged and maneuvered past the target at the correct angle.
The surgeon carefully removes the vessel loop while ensuring the lung is well exposed without applying excessive tension.
The surgeon gently pushes the aorta downward, creating space and improving visibility in the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes the XI L lymph node in the posterior area and then shifts focus to the anterior region to address the lingular branch.
The surgeon retracts tissue inferiorly and anteriorly to expose the lingular branch, revealing its position relative to surrounding structures.
The surgeon performs an aggressive lymph node dissection, skeletonizing the vessel by carefully mobilizing and removing surrounding lymph nodes.
The surgeon manipulates the level eleven L lymph node located between the lingular artery and the bronchus, facilitating easier passage of the surgical stapler.
The surgeon encounters large bronchial vessels, which may occasionally bleed during the dissection process.
The surgeon carefully dissects small connective tissue fibers, separating them from surrounding structures in a vascular area.
The surgeon uses a spreading technique to further separate connective tissue fibers, aiming to improve visualization of the dissection planes.
The surgeon uses a stapling device from the anterior approach to secure tissue during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon divides posterior structures from the anterior approach, facilitating clearer visualization of the dissection planes.
The surgeon approaches the anterior structures from the back, focusing on dividing these structures during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon positions the stapler at a nearly straight angle to facilitate the division of structures during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon adjusts the stapler to a slight fifteen-degree bend at the elbow, optimizing the angle for the procedure.
The surgeon visually inspects the superior pulmonary vein, which is exposed in the surgical field.
The surgeon retracts the lung posteriorly and dissects lymph node tissue located between the veins.
The surgical field shows the area where the lingular artery was previously located, with surrounding anatomical structures visible.
The surgeon continues to clean and dissect lymph node tissue located between the veins, facilitating easier access for subsequent steps in the procedure.
The surgeon prepares the surgical field for the later division of the superior pulmonary vein, with the lung retracted posteriorly and lymph node tissue dissected between the veins.
The surgeon encounters a lymph node within the surgical field, which appears prominent and may require removal or manipulation.
The surgeon mobilizes surrounding tissue to create a large space, carefully moving bronchio vessels aside to ensure unobstructed access for the surgical stepper.
The surgeon uses a stapler to secure the vessel, which helps maintain the created surgical window.
The surgeon has separated the two lobes by identifying and isolating the posterior fissure, anterior fissure, lingular artery, and posterior segment artery.
The lower lobe of the lung is visibly detached from surrounding structures, indicating completion of the separation process.

The surgeon retracts inferiorly to expose the vein and the first branch of the artery, performing superior mediastinal dissection. The mediastinal pleura overlying the hilum is dissected, with level five and six lymph nodes left for later.
The surgeon dissects level five and six lymph nodes, ensuring they are easily accessible and free of bleeding, while carefully separating them from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully manipulates tissue to maintain clear visualization, with the vagus nerve visible in the background.
The surgeon positions the instruments precisely on the pulmonary artery and along the edge of the superior pulmonary vein, ensuring clear visualization of these vascular structures.
The surgeon carefully navigates around the superior pulmonary vein and identifies the first branch of the pulmonary artery, ensuring precise dissection in this area.
The surgeon carefully identifies the plane adjacent to the vessel, meticulously removing nodal tissue from the surrounding area.
The surgeon exposes the first pulmonary artery trunk, revealing multiple branches including the anterior segment branch and the apical branch.
The video shows the pulmonary artery with multiple branches, highlighting the size and extent of the artery, including the stump or trunk.
The surgeon meticulously cleans the tissue surrounding the pulmonary artery, ensuring clear exposure of the vessel.

The surgeon dissects tissue between the superior pulmonary vein and the main pulmonary artery, using a bipolar instrument for retraction. The blunter instrument is positioned in the background to assist with the dissection.
The surgeon uses a blunt instrument to navigate around the tissue between the superior pulmonary vein and the main pulmonary artery, carefully maneuvering to avoid damage to surrounding structures.
The surgeon performs dissection in the retro space between the upper lobar bronchus and the vein, carefully separating these anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses a pinot dissector to sweep tissue into the jaws of the instrument, manipulating the tissue within the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully avoids the back wall of the vein while using a peanut dissector to gently manipulate the tissue, ensuring a smooth dissection without applying excessive force.
The surgeon prepares to grab the vessel loop, likely to encircle or manipulate a vessel during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon uses a vessel loop to maintain exposure of the surgical field, ensuring clear visibility of the anatomical structures during the thymectomy procedure.
The assistant trocar is visually aligned with the surgeon's arm number three, which is equipped with a bipolar instrument.
The assistant docks the robotic arm number three to the posteriorly placed twelve millimeter trocar, adjusting the angle for optimal surgical access.
The surgeon maneuvers the stapler around the vein, avoiding contact with the artery, using an optimal angle that requires minimal bending of the instrument.
The surgeon focuses on a level eleven lymph node, which is crucial for a successful left upper lobectomy, after completing the vein dissection.

The surgeon positions the vein stump cephalad to the upper lobe bronchus, inferior to the pulmonary artery, and below the recently divided vein stump. By rolling the vein stump distally into the lung, the lymph node becomes exposed.
The surgeon rolls the vein off the artery and adjacent lymph node, creating a larger window for further dissection.

The surgeon carefully manipulates and removes the level eleven lymph node, which is situated cephalad to the upper lobe bronchus and inferior to the pulmonary artery, ensuring clear access to the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts to mobilize a lymph node adjacent to the main pulmonary artery, performing dissection from the posterior aspect to facilitate removal.

The surgeon performs dissection to create a wide open window, revealing the lymph node coming off the bronchus. The back wall of the first pulmonary artery branch is visible, surrounded by thin areolar tissue, which is gently spread using a surgical instrument.
The surgeon mobilizes the vein and lymph nodes, extending the dissection further north or peripherally to improve access and visibility in the surgical field.

The surgeon adjusts the camera to visualize the window where the main pulmonary artery is visible. The camera is retracted slightly, and the surgeon performs a side-to-side movement to ensure no unintended contact with surrounding structures.
The surgeon's left hand is visible, indicating the entry point for the stapler, which will be used in the procedure.
The surgeon locates a vessel loop within the surgical field, preparing to manipulate or secure it as part of the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon adjusts the retraction technique to visualize the tip of the instrument after feeling it pass through the tissue.
The surgeon retracts further to expose the bronchus, clearing surrounding lymph nodes to facilitate the procedure.
The surgeon positions the bipolar instrument in the correct orientation for the next surgical step.
The surgeon staples the bronchus at the front, positioning the staple line close to and facing the pulmonary artery.
The surgeon uses the posterior hand to retract upward while the left hand retracts lower and anteriorly. An assistant positions a stapler in the surgical field.
The surgeon maneuvers the stapler, ensuring it is perfectly aligned to avoid the pulmonary artery. The stapler is positioned past the front end, closed down, and fired.
The surgeon has completed the skeletonization of the lymph nodes, revealing their distinct structures and surrounding tissue within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the lingular artery, posterior segment artery, a truncus branch feeding the apical anterior segments, along with the vein and bronchus in the surgical field.
The surgeon prepares to take the vein, which will facilitate lifting the surrounding parenchyma during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon dissects off the bronchus and proceeds to identify the artery, aiming to visualize it from the anterior approach. The pulmonary vein is being taken from the posterior port side.

The surgeon identifies and clears the level eleven lymph node situated between the stump of the pulmonary vein, the divided left upper lobe bronchus, and the main pulmonary artery. This action opens the surgical window for further division of these vessels.
The surgeon identifies a small vessel branch and carefully dissects periaventusial tissue to roll off lymph node tissue, facilitating easier removal.
The surgeon identifies the anterior segment branch, which is positioned lower down in the surgical field.
The surgeon navigates around a vessel from the posterior aspect, focusing on the periaventusial tissue to facilitate the removal of lymph node tissue.
The surgeon identifies a vein and its first branch, followed by the discovery of another smaller branch in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies three individual branches of a vein in the surgical field, highlighting their distinct separation.
The surgeon has isolated the vein and several arteries, preparing to address these structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins dissecting the anterior fissure, carefully separating the tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon retracts the lung posteriorly, revealing the bronchus and the eleven L lymph node after the vein has been taken.
The surgeon addresses the absence of a fissure by taking the vein and excising the lymph node, focusing on the anatomical structures visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon navigates a bloody surgical field to locate the lingual artery branch of the pulmonary artery (PA), using this landmark to complete the fissure. The bronchus is visible in the surgical window, following the ligation of two arteries.
The surgeon exposes the bronchus, revealing its anatomical position and surrounding structures.
The surgeon lifts the bronchial stump after taking the bronchus, allowing for continued dissection and manipulation of the surrounding lymph nodes.
The surgeon exposes a clear view of the lingular artery, showing its bifurcation into segments four and five.

The surgeon mobilizes additional lymph nodes near the posterior trocar stapling sites. The assistant uses a trocar to align with arm number three, facilitating the passage of the bipolar instrument around a branch that is difficult to access from the front.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes the anterior-posterior window lymph nodes, taking precautions to minimize bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully mobilizes the nodes without grabbing them until they are fully free, then proceeds to pull them out and dissect them with precision.
The surgeon carefully dissects a lymph node, separating it from surrounding tissue using precise surgical techniques.
The surgeon begins creating a tunnel-like space between anatomical structures, likely using careful dissection techniques to separate tissues.
The pulmonary artery is visible in the surgical field, providing a clear anatomical landmark during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies a poorly defined fissure and begins lifting the lung parenchyma off the pulmonary artery, creating a visible tunnel as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon continues to lift the parenchyma off the pulmonary artery, gradually creating a tunnel-like space. This process involves multiple stapling loads to simplify the approach and avoid the more challenging bronchus maneuver.
The surgeon carefully dissects the tissue in small increments to avoid damaging the underlying artery, ensuring precise control during the procedure.
